- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled cloud agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring cloud agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Fable, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

160	def _quantile(sorted_values: list[float], q: float) -> float:
161	    """Linear-interpolation quantile (``q`` in [0, 1]) over already-sorted values."""
162	    if not 0.0 <= q <= 1.0:
163	        raise ValueError(f"quantile q must be in [0, 1]; got {q}")
164	    n = len(sorted_values)
165	    if n == 1:
166	        return sorted_values[0]
167	    pos = q * (n - 1)
168	    lo = math.floor(pos)
169	    hi = math.ceil(pos)
170	    if lo == hi:
171	        return sorted_values[lo]
172	    frac = pos - lo
173	    return sorted_values[lo] * (1 - frac) + sorted_values[hi] * frac

176	def descriptive_stats(values: Sequence[float]) -> dict[str, float]:
177	    """Descriptive statistics of ``values``.
178	
179	    Returns ``n`` (count), ``mean``, ``std`` (sample std, ddof=1; ``0.0`` for a single value
180	    rather than NaN), ``min``, ``max``, and ``q25``/``median``/``q75`` (linear-interpolation
181	    quantiles). Raises on an empty input or any non-finite value.
182	    """
183	    if not values:
184	        raise ValueError("descriptive_stats requires at least one value")
185	    n = len(values)
186	    for v in values:
187	        if not math.isfinite(v):
188	            raise ValueError(f"values must be finite; got {v}")
189	    mean = math.fsum(values) / n
190	    if n == 1:
191	        std = 0.0
192	    else:
193	        var = math.fsum((v - mean) ** 2 for v in values) / (n - 1)
194	        std = math.sqrt(var)
195	    s = sorted(values)
196	    return {
197	        "n": float(n),
198	        "mean": mean,
199	        "std": std,
200	        "min": s[0],
201	        "max": s[-1],
202	        "q25": _quantile(s, 0.25),
203	        "median": _quantile(s, 0.5),

205	    }

98	
99	    Includes ``hypothesis_id``: although it is a label, it is fixed at lock time and is the key
100	    the runners attribute verdicts by (``verdict.hypothesis_id``), so leaving it unbound would
101	    let a post-lock id swap file a verdict under an id the proof never attested (adversarial

103	    they attest *to* this digest, so binding them would be circular).
104

134	class LocalHmacAuthority:
135	    """A real, on-box timestamp authority binding a digest and an instant under a secret key.
136	
137	    ``stamp`` issues ``local-hmac:v1:<instant-iso>|<hmac-hex>`` where the MAC is
138	    ``HMAC-SHA256(secret, digest || instant)``. ``verify`` recomputes the MAC over the digest
139	    the engine derived from the *current* hypothesis and the instant carried in the proof, and
140	    constant-time compares. The proof therefore verifies iff the content is unchanged, the
141	    instant is unchanged, and the same secret is used — without the secret it cannot be forged
142	    for arbitrary content or time.
143	
144	    Honest scope: this is local tamper-evidence, not third-party non-repudiation — the holder of
145	    ``secret`` could backdate (see module docstring). Use an RFC-3161 authority when you need a
146	    third party to vouch for the time.

152	        if not isinstance(secret, (bytes, bytearray)) or len(secret) < 16:
153	            raise ValueError("LocalHmacAuthority secret must be >= 16 bytes of key material")
154	        self._secret = bytes(secret)

157	        msg = canonical_json(["local-hmac:v1", digest, instant_iso]).encode("utf-8")

163	        instant_iso = when.isoformat()

167	        if not proof.startswith(_PROOF_PREFIX):

170	            )

173	        if not sep or not instant_iso or not mac_hex:
174	            raise PreRegistrationError("malformed local-hmac proof (expected <instant>|<mac>)")

219	def _submit_bounded(fn: Callable[[], Any]) -> Any:
220	    """Submit to the shared pool, refusing if every worker slot is already in flight
221	    (hung backend calls would otherwise leak all slots)."""
222	    global _inflight
223	    with _inflight_lock:
224	        if _inflight >= _POOL_WORKERS:
225	            # Saturation is a TRANSIENT condition — surface a retryable backpressure signal so
226	            # _run_with_retries waits and re-tries rather than failing the caller permanently
227	            # (#104). Concurrent callers thus queue briefly instead of erroring out.
228	            raise RateLimitError(
229	                f"reasoning timeout pool saturated ({_inflight}/{_POOL_WORKERS} in-flight) — "
230	                "backpressure; retry shortly"
231	            )
232	        _inflight += 1
233	    try:
234	        future = _pool.submit(fn)
235	    except RuntimeError as exc:  # pool shut down at interpreter exit — normalize, don't leak (#116)
236	        with _inflight_lock:
237	            _inflight -= 1
238	        raise PermanentReasoningError(f"reasoning pool is shut down: {exc}") from exc
239	    except BaseException:
240	        with _inflight_lock:
241	            _inflight -= 1
242	        raise
243	    # Register the slot-release callback under its own guard (#F-032): if a BaseException
244	    # (KeyboardInterrupt/SystemExit) fires while attaching the callback, release the slot
245	    # explicitly so a permanent leak can't accumulate and brick the pool with spurious
246	    # RateLimitErrors. If the interrupt landed AFTER add_done_callback already appended the
247	    # callback (so it will still fire on completion), the explicit _release here and the later
248	    # callback both target this future — _release is per-future idempotent, so the slot is
249	    # released exactly once either way.

564	def _sleep_bounded(secs: float, deadline: float | None) -> None:
565	    """Sleep ``secs``, but never past ``deadline`` — so backoff can't blow an overall budget.
566	
567	    If the deadline has already passed, raise instead of sleeping the full interval, so a long
568	    rate-limit backoff cannot keep a caller blocked well beyond its deadline (#102).
569	    """
570	    if deadline is not None:
571	        remaining = deadline - time.monotonic()
572	        if remaining <= 0:
573	            raise ReasoningError("reasoning deadline exceeded during backoff")
574	        secs = min(secs, remaining)
575	    time.sleep(secs)

578	def _run_with_retries(request: ReasoningRequest, *, deadline: float | None = None) -> str:
579	    """Execute with independent transient and rate-limit retry budgets.
580	
581	    ``deadline`` (monotonic seconds) bounds both the cumulative backoff AND entry into each
582	    retry: once the deadline has passed, no further ``_attempt`` is started (#102, #F-022). It
583	    does not interrupt an attempt already in flight — that is bounded by the per-call timeout —
584	    so total wall-clock is at most ``deadline`` plus one in-flight attempt's timeout, not
585	    ``deadline`` plus the full retry budget of per-call timeouts.
586	    """
587	    transient = 0
588	    rate = 0
589	    attempted = False
590	    while True:
591	        # Gate retry ENTRY on the deadline (#F-022): the first attempt always runs, but a retry is
592	        # not started once the deadline is past — otherwise the per-attempt timeouts could stack
593	        # well beyond the overall budget even though the backoff sleeps were bounded.
594	        if attempted and deadline is not None and time.monotonic() >= deadline:
595	            raise ReasoningError("reasoning deadline exceeded before retry")
596	        attempted = True
597	        try:
598	            return _attempt(request)
599	        except PermanentReasoningError:
600	            raise
601	        except RateLimitError:
602	            if rate >= RATE_LIMIT_MAX_RETRIES:
603	                raise
604	            _sleep_bounded(RATE_LIMIT_BACKOFF * (rate + 1), deadline)
605	            rate += 1
606	        except ReasoningError:
607	            if transient >= MAX_RETRIES:
608	                raise
609	            _sleep_bounded(BACKOFF_BASE**transient, deadline)
610	            transient += 1

105	def _sanitize_conn_str(conn_str: str) -> str:
106	    """Return ``scheme://host[:port]/path`` with any ``user:password`` userinfo stripped.
107	
108	    Robust to malformed userinfo: a password containing ``/``/``#`` makes ``urlsplit``'s
109	    ``.port`` raise ``ValueError`` on access, so the whole parse (including attribute access)
110	    is guarded and falls back to the generic userinfo-strip regex / a constant placeholder.
111	    """
112	    try:
113	        u = urlsplit(conn_str)
114	        host = u.hostname or ""
115	        port = u.port
116	        scheme = u.scheme
117	        path = u.path
118	    except ValueError:
119	        return _CREDS_RE.sub("://", conn_str)
120	    if not host:
121	        return _CREDS_RE.sub("://", conn_str) if scheme else "<store>"
122	    if ":" in host:  # IPv6 literal — urlsplit drops the brackets; restore them
123	        host = f"[{host}]"
124	    netloc = f"{host}:{port}" if port else host
125	    return f"{scheme}://{netloc}{path}" if scheme else "<store>"

128	def redact_creds(text: str, conn_str: str = "") -> str:
129	    """Remove credentials a backend exception may embed in its message text.
130	
131	    Layers, in order of precision:
132	
133	    1. exact replacement of the known ``conn_str`` (handles any password characters, incl.
134	       ``/`` and spaces) — this is the precise layer and covers the real connection string;
135	    2. a generic URI-userinfo strip (``://user:pass@`` → ``://``) as a backstop for any
136	       *other* URI in the text;
137	    3. a libpq DSN ``password=``/``pgpassword=`` strip for the keyword/value form.
138	
139	    Layers 2–3 are best-effort for *secondary* endpoints (e.g. a replica DSN) the caller did
140	    not pass as ``conn_str``.
141	
142	    Precision caveat (pass 5, #H-023): layer 1 is an EXACT-substring replacement, so it scrubs the
143	    primary connection string precisely only when that string appears verbatim in ``text``. If a
144	    backend reformats it (re-quoting, reordering DSN keywords, percent-encoding the password) the
145	    verbatim match misses and only the layers-2–3 regex backstops apply — which still strip
146	    ``://user:pass@`` userinfo and ``password=`` tokens, but are not guaranteed exhaustive for
147	    every backend message shape. It is "precise when the conn string appears verbatim", not
148	    "always precise".
149	    """
150	    if conn_str:
151	        text = text.replace(conn_str, _sanitize_conn_str(conn_str))
152	    text = _CREDS_RE.sub("://", text)
153	    return _DSN_PW_RE.sub(r"\1=***", text)

103	def build_baseline_artifact(
104	    corpus: Corpus,
105	    contents: Mapping[str, Any],
106	    *,
107	    component_versions: Mapping[str, str] | None = None,
108	    extra_inputs: Mapping[str, Any] | None = None,
109	    seed: int | None = None,
110	    corpus_hash: str | None = None,
111	    trust_corpus_hash: bool = False,
112	) -> BaselineArtifact:
113	    """Assemble a :class:`BaselineArtifact` with a complete determinism record.
114	
115	    ``contents`` is the builder's output (embeddings, similarity graph, clusters, …).
116	    ``component_versions`` records the builder + any model/library versions (the engine and
117	    Python versions are added automatically). ``extra_inputs`` are additional inputs to hash
118	    (e.g. config). ``seed``, if omitted, is derived deterministically from the corpus + inputs
119	    so it is reproducible.
120	
121	    ``corpus_hash`` lets a caller pass an already-computed :func:`corpus_fingerprint` (#119). By
122	    default it is **verified** against the corpus and a mismatch raises ``ValueError`` — a
123	    stale/typo'd hash would otherwise produce a determinism record whose fingerprint and derived
124	    seed describe a *different* corpus than the one passed, silently defeating the reproducibility
125	    guarantee this harness exists to provide (#155). Pass ``trust_corpus_hash=True`` to skip the
126	    recompute (the #119 optimization) only when the caller computed the hash from this exact
127	    corpus and accepts integrity responsibility.
128	    """
129	    if corpus_hash is None:
130	        corpus_hash = corpus_fingerprint(corpus)
131	    elif not trust_corpus_hash:
132	        # Verify the supplied hash binds the corpus actually passed (default-safe, #155).
133	        actual = corpus_fingerprint(corpus)
134	        if corpus_hash != actual:
135	            raise ValueError(
136	                f"corpus_hash {corpus_hash!r} does not match the corpus "
137	                f"(corpus_fingerprint={actual!r}) — the determinism record would bind the wrong "
138	                "data; pass trust_corpus_hash=True only if you accept integrity responsibility"
139	            )
140	    if extra_inputs and "corpus" in extra_inputs:
141	        raise ValueError("'corpus' is a reserved input-hash key — rename the extra input (#B7)")
142	    input_hashes = {"corpus": corpus_hash}
143	    for name, value in (extra_inputs or {}).items():
144	        # Name the offending key when an extra input is not canonicalizable (#G-021): hash_value
145	        # raises a raw ValueError (non-finite) / TypeError (unreproducible repr) with no context,
146	        # so a rich config with one bad value gave an opaque error. Re-raise with the key.
147	        try:
148	            input_hashes[name] = hash_value(value)
149	        except (ValueError, TypeError) as exc:
150	            raise type(exc)(f"extra_input {name!r} is not canonicalizable: {exc}") from exc
151	
152	    versions = {"engine": _ENGINE_VERSION, "python": sys.version.split()[0]}
153	    versions.update(component_versions or {})
154	
155	    if seed is None:
156	        # Derive from all input hashes (the corpus hash is already among them under "corpus").
157	        seed = stable_seed(*(input_hashes[k] for k in sorted(input_hashes)))
158	
159	    record = DeterminismRecord(seed=seed, input_hashes=input_hashes, component_versions=versions)
160	    return BaselineArtifact(
161	        corpus_fingerprint=corpus_hash, contents=contents, determinism=record.as_dict()
162	    )

1	"""Dependency-light, deterministic numeric primitives — the baseline's raw material.
2	
3	These are the small, exact, NumPy-free building blocks the methodology calls "raw material"
4	for a baseline: similarity/distance and descriptive statistics over a
5	:class:`~assay_engine.contracts.features.FeatureMatrix`. They are deterministic (no RNG, no
6	iteration-order dependence) and pure, so a study can assemble a simple baseline — and the
7	engine can unit-test these invariants — with no heavy dependency.
8	
9	Studies needing performance or richer ML (embedding models, clustering, graph/topology) bring
10	their own builders implementing :class:`~assay_engine.baseline.toolkit.BaselineBuilder`; the
11	engine deliberately does not prescribe those choices (ADR-0002). Heavier engine helpers may be
12	added later under the optional ``baseline`` extra, but the contract here stays dependency-free.
13	"""
14	
15	from __future__ import annotations
16	
17	import math
18	from typing import Any, Sequence
19	
20	Vector = Sequence[float]
21	
22	
23	def _check_same_length(a: Vector, b: Vector) -> None:
24	    if len(a) != len(b):
25	        raise ValueError(f"vectors must have equal length; got {len(a)} and {len(b)}")
26	
27	
28	def _check_finite(*vectors: Vector) -> None:
29	    """Reject non-finite components. Without this a NaN/inf could flow into cosine and be
30	    silently clamped to a false 1.0 (audit #B6); consistent with descriptive_stats."""
31	    for vec in vectors:
32	        for x in vec:
33	            if not math.isfinite(x):
34	                raise ValueError(f"vector components must be finite; got {x}")
35	
36	
37	def dot(a: Vector, b: Vector) -> float:
38	    _check_same_length(a, b)
39	    return math.fsum(x * y for x, y in zip(a, b))
40	
41	
42	def l2_norm(a: Vector) -> float:
43	    return math.sqrt(math.fsum(x * x for x in a))
44	
45	
46	def _clamp_unit(x: float) -> float:
47	    """Clamp to [-1, 1] to absorb floating-point overshoot (e.g. self-similarity 1.0000…002)."""
48	    return max(-1.0, min(1.0, x))
49	
50	
51	def cosine_similarity(a: Vector, b: Vector) -> float:
52	    """Cosine similarity in [-1, 1]. A zero vector has no direction, so its similarity to
53	    anything is defined as 0.0 (rather than NaN). The result is clamped to [-1, 1] so float
54	    rounding cannot push it outside the documented range (audit #B3)."""
55	    _check_same_length(a, b)
56	    _check_finite(a, b)
57	    na, nb = l2_norm(a), l2_norm(b)
58	    if na == 0.0 or nb == 0.0:
59	        return 0.0
60	    return _clamp_unit(dot(a, b) / (na * nb))
61	
62	
63	def euclidean_distance(a: Vector, b: Vector) -> float:
64	    _check_same_length(a, b)
65	    _check_finite(a, b)
66	    return math.sqrt(math.fsum((x - y) ** 2 for x, y in zip(a, b)))
67	
68	
69	def _cosine_matrix_pure(rows: Sequence[Vector]) -> list[list[float]]:
70	    """Pure-Python reference implementation — O(n²·d). Correct but slow; the public function
71	    dispatches to a numpy path at scale when available."""
72	    n = len(rows)
73	    _check_finite(*rows)
74	    norms = [l2_norm(r) for r in rows]
75	    out = [[0.0] * n for _ in range(n)]
76	    for i in range(n):

Interesting — there's a potential parity divergence. In `_cosine_matrix_pure`, the diagonal for a zero vector: line 78 checks `norms[i] == 0.0 or norms[j] == 0.0` FIRST, so a zero vector's self-similarity (i==j, norm 0) returns 0.0. But there is a real divergence: `_cosine_matrix_pure` does NOT check `x.ndim` or ragged rows the way numpy does. Actually `_check_same_length` isn't called in pure path. Let me verify: pure path calls `_check_finite(*rows)` then `l2_norm`, `dot`. `dot` calls `_check_same_length(rows[i], rows[j])`. So ragged would raise in dot for off-diagonal. But for a single row or all-same-length it's fine. Numpy path raises ValueError on ragged via ndim check. Pure path: `np.asarray` isn't used. For ragged rows, pure would raise ValueError from `dot`→`_check_same_length` only when comparing differing rows. Minor parity but both raise ValueError. Not a clear defect.

There's a subtle bug at line 144: `_digest(...)` is called and `entry_hash` is computed, then at line 146 the `ProvenanceEntry` is constructed. But `_record_locked` computes `entry_hash` over `payload` (the raw payload), while `ProvenanceEntry.__post_init__` freezes payload via `freeze_mapping`. 55	    if not is_loopback_host(host):
56	        raise NonLocalEndpointError(
57	            f"{what} must be a loopback host (ADR-0003 data sovereignty); got {host!r}"
58	        )
59	    return host
60	
61	
62	def _authority_hosts(netloc: str) -> list[str]:
63	    """Every host in a URI authority, supporting libpq's comma-separated multi-host form.
64	
65	    ``urlparse().hostname`` returns only the *first* host of a multi-host authority
66	    (``host1:port1,host2:port2``), so a remote second host would slip past a single-host
67	    check (audit #D3). Each host may carry its own ``:port`` and IPv6 hosts are bracketed.
68	
69	    Userinfo is stripped at the **first** ``@`` — matching libpq, which (unlike ``urlparse``,
70	    which uses the last ``@``) treats the first ``@`` as the userinfo delimiter. A string like
71	    ``[REDACTED]:5432@localhost`` is therefore read as host ``evil.com`` here, not
72	    ``localhost`` (audit #D5). Any host element still containing ``@`` after that is ambiguous
73	    and is returned verbatim so the caller's loopback check fails it closed.
74	    """
75	    if "@" in netloc:
76	        netloc = netloc.split("@", 1)[1]
77	    hosts: list[str] = []
78	    for part in netloc.split(","):
79	        p = part.strip()
80	        if not p:
81	            continue
82	        if p.startswith("["):  # [ipv6] or [ipv6]:port
83	            end = p.find("]")
84	            hosts.append(p[1:end] if end != -1 else p[1:])
85	        else:
86	            hosts.append(p.rsplit(":", 1)[0] if ":" in p else p)
87	    return hosts
88	
89	
90	def require_local_uri(uri: str, *, what: str) -> str:
91	    """Return ``uri`` if it is local (ADR-0003), else raise :class:`NonLocalEndpointError`.
92	
93	    Three forms are recognized, validated against what the real client (libpq for postgres,
94	    urllib for http, sqlite for files) actually connects to — not merely what ``urlparse``
95	    reports, since the two can disagree:
96	
97	    - **URI with an authority** (``scheme://host…`` or scheme-relative ``//host…``): *every*
98	      host in the authority must be loopback (incl. each of a comma-separated multi-host
99	      authority, #D3), AND any ``host``/``hostaddr`` query parameter that libpq would use to
100	      override the authority host (#D2). ``hostaddr`` is checked independently of ``host``.
101	    - **libpq keyword/value DSN** (no authority but ``key=value`` tokens): ``host``/``hostaddr``
102	      must be loopback, tolerating libpq's whitespace/quoting around ``=`` (#P1, #D1).
103	    - **bare path** (no authority, no ``=``): a local file/dir — but a Windows UNC path
104	      (``\\\\server\\share``) is rejected as remote.
105	
106	    Inputs that cannot be parsed safely fail closed (rejected), never accepted.
107	    """
108	    try:
109	        parsed = urlparse(uri)
110	        netloc = parsed.netloc
111	        query = parsed.query
112	    except ValueError:
113	        # e.g. a malformed IPv6 authority — we cannot determine the host, so refuse.
114	        raise NonLocalEndpointError(
115	            f"{what}: endpoint could not be parsed safely; rejecting (ADR-0003)"
116	        ) from None
117	
118	    if netloc:
119	        # A backslash never legitimately appears in a URI authority; a WHATWG-normalizing
120	        # client (e.g. some HTTP stacks) treats '\' as '/', so it could read a different host
121	        # than urlparse. Refuse rather than risk that differential (audit #D6, defense-in-depth).
122	        if "\\" in netloc:
123	            raise NonLocalEndpointError(
124	                f"{what}: backslash in URI authority is not permitted (ADR-0003); rejecting"
125	            )
126	        for h in _authority_hosts(netloc):
127	            # A residual '@' means the authority is ambiguous between this parser and libpq —
128	            # fail closed (audit #D5).
129	            if "@" in h or not is_loopback_host(h):
130	                raise NonLocalEndpointError(
131	                    f"{what} must be a local store (ADR-0003 data sovereignty); authority "
132	                    f"names non-loopback host {h!r}"
133	                )
134	        for key in ("host", "hostaddr"):
135	            for val in parse_qs(query).get(key, []):
136	                h = val.strip()
137	                if h and not is_loopback_host(h):
138	                    raise NonLocalEndpointError(
139	                        f"{what} must be a local store (ADR-0003 data sovereignty); URI query "
140	                        f"{key}={h!r} points off-box"
141	                    )
142	        return uri
143	
144	    if "=" in uri:  # libpq keyword/value DSN (no URI authority)
145	        for match in _DSN_TOKEN_RE.finditer(uri):
146	            if match.group(1).strip().lower() in {"host", "hostaddr"}:
147	                h = match.group(2).strip().strip("'\"")
148	                if h and not is_loopback_host(h):
149	                    raise NonLocalEndpointError(
150	                        f"{what} must be a local store (ADR-0003 data sovereignty); DSN names "
151	                        f"non-loopback host {h!r}"
152	                    )
153	        return uri
154	
155	    stripped = uri.lstrip()
156	    # Windows treats ANY two leading separators (in any mix of '\' and '/') as a UNC prefix
157	    # to a remote SMB share — \\srv, //srv, /\srv, \/srv all resolve off-box (audit #D6).
158	    # (The pure '//host' form is already caught by the authority branch above; covering it
159	    # here too keeps the branch self-contained.)
160	    if len(stripped) >= 2 and stripped[0] in "\\/" and stripped[1] in "\\/":
161	        raise NonLocalEndpointError(
162	            f"{what} must be a local store (ADR-0003 data sovereignty); UNC-style path "
163	            f"{uri!r} is a remote share"
164	        )
165	    return uri  # bare path / dir store
166

1	"""The measurement ↔ interpretation fence (METHODOLOGY.md §6).
2	
3	A hard boundary separates **measurement** (numbers the engine produces) from
4	**interpretation** (what they mean). Interpretation lives strictly downstream and cannot
5	feed back into measurement. Any use of judgment — including LLM-assisted classification — is
6	interpretation, and is labeled as such.
7	
8	These wrappers make the boundary explicit and type-visible in the pipeline. ``fence`` turns
9	a measurement into an interpretation; there is deliberately no inverse — you cannot turn an
10	interpretation back into a measurement.
11	"""
12	
13	from __future__ import annotations
14	
15	from collections.abc import Mapping as AbcMapping
16	from dataclasses import dataclass, field
17	from typing import Any, Generic, Mapping, TypeVar
18	
19	from assay_engine._frozen import duck_mapping_items, freeze, freeze_mapping
20	
21	T = TypeVar("T")
22	
23	
24	@dataclass(frozen=True, slots=True)
25	class Interpretation(Generic[T]):
26	    """A judgment *about* one or more measurements. Downstream-only; never an input upstream."""
27	
28	    value: T
29	    basis: tuple[str, ...]  # refs (producer:inputs_hash) of the measurements this interprets
30	    rationale: str
31	    judged_by: str  # e.g. "operator" or a model identifier — judgment is always labeled
32	    metadata: Mapping[str, Any] = field(default_factory=dict)
33	
34	    def __post_init__(self) -> None:
35	        # Freeze the payload so the frozen record is deeply immutable + hashable (issue #29).
36	        object.__setattr__(self, "value", freeze(self.value))
37	        object.__setattr__(self, "metadata", freeze_mapping(self.metadata))
38	
39	
40	def _contains_interpretation(obj: Any) -> bool:
41	    """True if ``obj`` is, or recursively contains, an :class:`Interpretation`."""
42	    if isinstance(obj, Interpretation):
43	        return True
44	    if isinstance(obj, AbcMapping):
45	        # scan KEYS as well as values — an Interpretation used as a dict key would otherwise be
46	        # smuggled into a Measurement undetected (#115)
47	        return any(
48	            _contains_interpretation(k) or _contains_interpretation(v) for k, v in obj.items()
49	        )
50	    duck = duck_mapping_items(obj)
51	    if duck is not None:  # lazy/proxy dict-like — scan it like a mapping, not an opaque leaf (#140)
52	        return any(_contains_interpretation(k) or _contains_interpretation(v) for k, v in duck)
53	    if isinstance(obj, (list, tuple, set, frozenset)):
54	        return any(_contains_interpretation(v) for v in obj)
55	    return False
56	
57	
58	@dataclass(frozen=True, slots=True)
59	class Measurement(Generic[T]):
60	    """A value produced purely by the engine from data — no judgment involved."""
61	
62	    value: T
63	    produced_by: str
64	    inputs_hash: str
65	    metadata: Mapping[str, Any] = field(default_factory=dict)
66	
67	    def __post_init__(self) -> None:
68	        # Structurally close the one-way fence: an interpretation may never re-enter as a
69	        # measurement value — at the top level OR nested in a container/metadata (issues #22,
70	        # #30). Note: ref/inputs_hash integrity (forgery resistance) is delegated to the
71	        # provenance/audit layer, not enforced here.
72	        if _contains_interpretation(self.value) or _contains_interpretation(self.metadata):
73	            raise TypeError(
74	                "Measurement value/metadata must not contain an Interpretation — "
75	                "interpretation must not feed back into measurement (the fence)"
76	            )
77	        # Freeze the payload so the frozen record is deeply immutable + hashable (issue #29).
78	        object.__setattr__(self, "value", freeze(self.value))
79	        object.__setattr__(self, "metadata", freeze_mapping(self.metadata))
80	
81	    @property
82	    def ref(self) -> str:
83	        """A stable id for this specific measurement: producer + input hash."""
84	        return f"{self.produced_by}:{self.inputs_hash}"
85	
86	
87	def fence(
88	    measurement: Measurement[Any], value: T, *, rationale: str, judged_by: str
89	) -> Interpretation[T]:
90	    """Cross the fence once, in the only permitted direction: measurement → interpretation.
91	
92	    ``basis`` records the specific measurement's ``ref`` (``producer:inputs_hash``), so an
93	    interpretation can be traced to the exact measurement it interprets — not merely to the
94	    producer (audit pass 1, issue #16).
95	    """
96	    return Interpretation(
97	        value=value,
98	        basis=(measurement.ref,),
99	        rationale=rationale,
100	        judged_by=judged_by,
101	        metadata={"inputs_hash": measurement.inputs_hash},
102	    )
103

1	"""Canonical schema — the dataset-agnostic shape the baseline is built from.
2	
3	An adapter's job is to turn its raw source into a :class:`Corpus` of these types. The engine
4	builds the baseline from a ``Corpus`` and never sees the source's native format.
5	
6	Firewall A boundary (an adapter obligation, not a type-enforced guarantee):
7	    A ``Corpus`` carries the *data itself* — units of text/attributes and any structure that
8	    is intrinsic to the raw source. It must **not** carry an external authority's *judgments*
9	    about the data (asserted relationships, strengths, labels, a taxonomy). Those are claims;
10	    they travel through :mod:`assay_engine.contracts.claims`, quarantined from baseline
11	    construction. Putting an external mapping into ``Corpus.relations`` (or external labels
12	    into ``attributes``/``metadata``) is a Firewall A violation.
13	
14	    Because the mapping/relation payloads are deliberately open (the engine is
15	    dataset-agnostic), this boundary is an **adapter contract obligation** the type system
16	    cannot enforce — not a structural guarantee. The structural guarantees of Firewall A are
17	    that the baseline builder is never handed an ``ExternalClaimsSource`` and that
18	    ``StudyDefinition`` refuses a claims source outside adjudication mode (audit pass 1,
19	    issues #1, #3).
20	"""
21	
22	from __future__ import annotations
23	
24	from dataclasses import dataclass, field
25	from typing import Any, Mapping
26	
27	from assay_engine._frozen import freeze_mapping
28	
29	
30	@dataclass(frozen=True, slots=True)
31	class Unit:
32	    """One analyzable object in the corpus (a document, record, entity, item).
33	
34	    ``unit_id`` is stable and unique within the corpus. ``text`` is the natural-language
35	    content the NLP baseline operates on (may be empty for non-text units). ``attributes``
36	    holds structured fields intrinsic to the data — never an external authority's verdicts.
37	    """
38	
39	    unit_id: str
40	    text: str = ""
41	    attributes: Mapping[str, Any] = field(default_factory=dict)
42	    source_ref: str | None = None
43	
44	    def __post_init__(self) -> None:
45	        object.__setattr__(self, "attributes", freeze_mapping(self.attributes))
46	
47	
48	@dataclass(frozen=True, slots=True)
49	class Relation:
50	    """A relationship that is *intrinsic to the raw data* (not an external judgment).
51	
52	    Example of legitimate use: a parent/child link the source file itself encodes. Example of
53	    illegitimate use: an external expert's asserted mapping between units — that is a claim
54	    (see :mod:`assay_engine.contracts.claims`) and must be withheld from the baseline.
55	    """
56	
57	    source_id: str
58	    target_id: str
59	    kind: str
60	    attributes: Mapping[str, Any] = field(default_factory=dict)
61	
62	    def __post_init__(self) -> None:
63	        object.__setattr__(self, "attributes", freeze_mapping(self.attributes))
64	
65	
66	@dataclass(frozen=True, slots=True)
67	class Corpus:
68	    """The full canonical dataset handed to the baseline builder."""
69	
70	    units: tuple[Unit, ...]
71	    relations: tuple[Relation, ...] = ()
72	    metadata: Mapping[str, Any] = field(default_factory=dict)
73	
74	    def unit_ids(self) -> frozenset[str]:
75	        return frozenset(u.unit_id for u in self.units)
76	
77	    def __post_init__(self) -> None:
78	        ids = [u.unit_id for u in self.units]
79	        if len(ids) != len(set(ids)):
80	            raise ValueError("Corpus contains duplicate unit_id values")
81	        # A closed canonical corpus must not contain relations to non-existent units
82	        # (audit pass 1, issue #3) — mirrors the duplicate-id and FeatureMatrix checks.
83	        known = set(ids)
84	        dangling = sorted(
85	            {e for r in self.relations for e in (r.source_id, r.target_id) if e not in known}
86	        )
87	        if dangling:
88	            raise ValueError(f"Corpus has relations referencing unknown unit_id(s): {dangling[:5]}")
89	        object.__setattr__(self, "metadata", freeze_mapping(self.metadata))

230	    resamples: Sequence[float], null: Sequence[float], tail: Direction, alpha: float
231	) -> float:
232	    """Fraction of resamples that **reproduce the observed effect**.
233	
234	    Each resample is scored as its own observed statistic against the null and must itself be
235	    significant in the predicted tail (empirical p ≤ ``alpha``). This measures reproduction of
236	    the observed *effect* — not merely landing on the predicted side of the null center, which a
237	    resample distribution clearing the null median by an epsilon would satisfy while reproducing
238	    nothing of the observed magnitude (audit pass 2, #139). The closer to 1.0, the more
239	    consistently the effect's significance reproduces across resamples.
240	
241	    The default path is O(R·N) pure Python (one null pass per resample). When numpy is available
242	    (the optional ``baseline`` extra) the whole R×N comparison runs as a single vectorized op
243	    (pass 3, #F-017). The numpy path computes the IDENTICAL integer extreme-counts and the same
244	    ``(extreme + 1) / (n + 1) <= alpha`` test, so its result is exactly equal to the Python path;
245	    it is purely a speed optimization for large R·N, never a semantic change.
246	    """
247	    n = len(null)
248	    try:
249	        import numpy as np
250	
251	        # Use searchsorted on a sorted null (O((R+N) log N) time, O(R) memory) rather than the
252	        # R×N broadcast boolean matrix (#G-010): the matrix peaks at ~R*N bytes (250MB for
253	        # R=5000,N=50000) and can OOM where the pure-Python loop would not. The integer
254	        # extreme-counts are IDENTICAL to _empirical_p: for 'greater', count(null >= r) = N -
255	        # searchsorted(sorted, r, 'left'); for 'less', count(null <= r) = searchsorted(sorted, r,
256	        # 'right'). Then (extreme+1)/(n+1) <= alpha, same as the pure path.
257	        sorted_null = np.sort(np.asarray(null, dtype=np.float64))
258	        res_arr = np.asarray(resamples, dtype=np.float64)
259	        if tail == "greater":
260	            extreme = n - np.searchsorted(sorted_null, res_arr, side="left")
261	        else:
262	            extreme = np.searchsorted(sorted_null, res_arr, side="right")
263	        p = (extreme + 1) / (n + 1)
264	        return float(int((p <= alpha).sum()) / len(resamples))
265	    except ImportError:
266	        significant = sum(1 for r in resamples if _empirical_p(null, r, tail) <= alpha)
267	        return significant / len(resamples)

129	def freeze_mapping(value: Mapping[str, Any]) -> FrozenDict:
130	    """Normalize a mapping field to a deep-immutable :class:`FrozenDict`.
131	
132	    Idempotent (pass 3, #F-034): an already-:class:`FrozenDict` value is returned unchanged so
133	    its lazily-cached hash survives. Re-wrapping (e.g. when ``ProvenanceEntry.__post_init__``
134	    freezes a payload that already holds FrozenDict values) would otherwise allocate a fresh
135	    FrozenDict with ``_hash=None`` and force an O(N·depth) re-hash on next use.
136	    """
137	    if isinstance(value, FrozenDict):
138	        return value
139	    return FrozenDict({k: freeze(v) for k, v in value.items()})

142	def unfreeze(value: Any) -> Any:
143	    """Recursively convert a frozen structure back into plain JSON-serializable containers.
144	
145	    The inverse of :func:`freeze` for serialization: :class:`FrozenDict` → ``dict``,
146	    ``frozenset`` → sorted ``list`` (stable order for reproducible output), ``tuple`` →
147	    ``list``. Leaves are returned unchanged. ``json.dumps`` does not know :class:`FrozenDict`
148	    (it is a Mapping, not a ``dict`` subclass), so an operator serializing a deep-frozen public
149	    payload — e.g. ``GateReview.payload`` — must thaw it first (#141).
150	    """
151	    if isinstance(value, (FrozenDict, Mapping)):
152	        return {k: unfreeze(v) for k, v in value.items()}
153	    if isinstance(value, frozenset):
154	        # sort for reproducible output; when elements are not mutually orderable (mixed types),
155	        # fall back to a STABLE key — (type name, repr) — rather than raw set iteration order, which
156	        # is hash-seed/insertion dependent and would make serialized output non-reproducible across
157	        # processes (pass 4, #G-004).
158	        try:
159	            return [unfreeze(v) for v in sorted(value)]
160	        except TypeError:
161	            return [unfreeze(v) for v in sorted(value, key=lambda x: (type(x).__name__, repr(x)))]
162	    if isinstance(value, (list, tuple)):
163	        return [unfreeze(v) for v in value]
164	    return value

291	def _resolve_direction(hypothesis: Hypothesis, predicted_direction: Direction | None) -> Direction:
292	    """Direction is fixed at lock time on the hypothesis (issue #24); the confirm-time argument is
293	    optional and, if given, must MATCH the pre-registered one — it can cross-check, never supply.
294	
295	    A hypothesis reaching here has already passed the confirm gate, so it is locked and its
296	    ``predicted_direction`` is part of the cryptographic binding. A ``None`` locked direction
297	    therefore means the tail was *not* pre-registered; accepting a confirm-time direction in that
298	    case would let the analyst pick the tail AFTER seeing the data (HARKing), flipping
299	    supported↔contradicted on a hypothesis whose lock attests "no direction" (audit pass 4,
300	    #G-001). The tail must be committed before confirmation, so a directionless locked hypothesis
301	    is refused outright rather than completed with a post-hoc direction.
302	
303	    The resolved direction is validated against ``{"greater", "less"}``; any other value is
304	    rejected (an unrecognized tail would silently fall through to 'less' in :func:`_empirical_p`,
305	    flipping the verdict — audit pass 2, #128).
306	    """
307	    locked_dir = hypothesis.predicted_direction
308	    if locked_dir is None:
309	        raise ValueError(
310	            "predicted_direction was not pre-registered: a whole-corpus confirmatory hypothesis "
311	            "must lock its tail ('greater' or 'less') BEFORE confirmation. Supplying a confirm-time "
312	            "direction for a directionless locked hypothesis selects the tail after seeing the data "
313	            "(HARKing) and is refused (#G-001)."
314	        )
315	    if predicted_direction is not None and predicted_direction != locked_dir:
316	        raise ValueError(
317	            "predicted_direction argument contradicts the hypothesis's pre-registered "
318	            f"direction ({predicted_direction!r} != {locked_dir!r})"
319	        )
320	    resolved = locked_dir
321	    if resolved not in _VALID_DIRECTIONS:
322	        raise ValueError(
323	            f"predicted_direction must be one of {_VALID_DIRECTIONS}; got {resolved!r} — an "
324	            "unrecognized tail would silently flip the supported/contradicted verdict"
325	        )

351	    - significant in the *opposite* tail → ``contradicted``;

1	"""The three verdicts (ADR-0004, METHODOLOGY.md §4).
2	
3	A confirmatory test returns exactly one of supported / contradicted / indeterminate — never
4	a forced binary. Every verdict carries the evidence and the decision rule behind it, so a
5	reviewer can re-derive it.
6	"""
7	
8	from __future__ import annotations
9	
10	from dataclasses import dataclass, field
11	from enum import Enum
12	from typing import Any, Mapping
13	
14	from assay_engine._frozen import freeze_mapping
15	
16	
17	class VerdictLabel(Enum):
18	    SUPPORTED = "supported"
19	    CONTRADICTED = "contradicted"
20	    INDETERMINATE = "indeterminate"
21	
22	
23	@dataclass(frozen=True, slots=True)
24	class Verdict:
25	    """The outcome of one confirmatory test, structured identically for all three labels.
26	
27	    ``indeterminate`` is first-class: underpowered, beyond the measurement's reach, or a
28	    disagreement plausibly attributable to a method limitation rather than a claim error.
29	    """
30	
31	    hypothesis_id: str
32	    label: VerdictLabel
33	    decision_rule: str
34	    statistic: float | None = None
35	    threshold: float | None = None
36	    evidence: Mapping[str, Any] = field(default_factory=dict)
37	    notes: str = ""
38	
39	    def __post_init__(self) -> None:
40	        # The label annotation is type-checker-only; a study-supplied confirmer (the public
41	        # ClaimConfirmer/HeldOutConfirmer Protocols) that returns a bare string label would
42	        # otherwise build a structurally-valid Verdict that later crashes _score with an opaque
43	        # KeyError and breaks verdict.label.value provenance lines. Fail fast and attributable
44	        # at construction instead (#133).
45	        if not isinstance(self.label, VerdictLabel):
46	            raise TypeError(
47	                f"Verdict.label must be a VerdictLabel, got {type(self.label).__name__!r} "
48	                f"({self.label!r}) for hypothesis {self.hypothesis_id!r}"
49	            )
50	        if not isinstance(self.hypothesis_id, str) or not self.hypothesis_id:
51	            raise ValueError("Verdict.hypothesis_id must be a non-empty str")
52	        object.__setattr__(self, "evidence", freeze_mapping(self.evidence))
53	
54	    @property
55	    def is_indeterminate(self) -> bool:
56	        return self.label is VerdictLabel.INDETERMINATE
57	
58	    @classmethod
59	    def supported(cls, hypothesis_id: str, decision_rule: str, **kw: Any) -> "Verdict":
60	        return cls(hypothesis_id, VerdictLabel.SUPPORTED, decision_rule, **kw)
61	
62	    @classmethod
63	    def contradicted(cls, hypothesis_id: str, decision_rule: str, **kw: Any) -> "Verdict":
64	        return cls(hypothesis_id, VerdictLabel.CONTRADICTED, decision_rule, **kw)
65	
66	    @classmethod
67	    def indeterminate(cls, hypothesis_id: str, decision_rule: str, **kw: Any) -> "Verdict":
68	        return cls(hypothesis_id, VerdictLabel.INDETERMINATE, decision_rule, **kw)

84	    # A significance threshold of 0.5 or more is not a meaningful one-sided level, and
85	    # admitting it lets both tails register "significant" at once (alpha < 0.5 guarantees
86	    # p_support + p_contra >= 1 cannot both fall below alpha) — fix-review nit on issue #2.
87	    if not (0.0 < alpha < 0.5):
88	        raise ValueError(f"alpha must be in (0, 0.5); got {alpha}")
89	
90

92	    if not (0.0 <= p_value <= 1.0):
93	        raise ValueError(f"p_value must be in [0, 1]; got {p_value}")
94	
95	
96	def _validate_finite(name: str, value: float) -> None:
97	    if not math.isfinite(value):
98	        raise ValueError(f"{name} must be a finite number; got {value}")
99

101	def verdict_from_pvalue(
102	    hypothesis_id: str,
103	    *,
104	    statistic: float,
105	    p_value: float,
106	    alpha: float,
107	    powered: bool,
108	    direction_supports_claim: bool,
109	    test_description: str = "confirmatory test",
110	) -> Verdict:
111	    """Map a confirmatory statistic onto the three verdicts.
112	
113	    - not ``powered`` → ``indeterminate`` (the method cannot decide).
114	    - significant and direction agrees with the claim → ``supported``.
115	    - significant and direction disagrees → ``contradicted``.
116	    - not significant → ``indeterminate`` (absence of evidence is not contradiction here).
117	
118	    ``p_value`` and ``direction_supports_claim`` must be derived by the caller from a test
119	    appropriate to the claim's predicted direction. For a unit-level held-out test the engine
120	    is handed only an opaque ``statistic`` + ``p_value`` and cannot itself infer whether the
121	    statistic supports or contradicts the claim — so ``direction_supports_claim`` is a trusted
122	    caller obligation, ``statistic`` is recorded for provenance only (it does NOT gate the
123	    verdict), and the deciding flag is recorded in ``evidence`` so the verdict is re-derivable
124	    (audit pass 2, issue #20). Inputs are validated (issue #4) and the recorded decision rule
125	    reflects the caller's ``test_description``, not a hard-coded sidedness label (issue #2).
126	
127	    ``powered`` is likewise a TRUSTED CALLER OBLIGATION (pass 5, #H-002): the engine cannot
128	    compute statistical power from an opaque statistic/p-value, so the caller asserts whether the
129	    test was adequately powered (``powered=False`` forces ``indeterminate``). The engine cannot
130	    verify it but records it in ``evidence`` so the verdict's dependence on the flag is auditable.
131	    """
132	    _validate_pvalue(p_value)
133	    _validate_alpha(alpha)
134	    _validate_finite("statistic", statistic)
135	
136	    rule = f"{test_description} at alpha={alpha}; powered={powered}"
137	    common = dict(
138	        statistic=statistic,
139	        threshold=alpha,
140	        evidence={
141	            "p_value": p_value,
142	            "direction_supports_claim": direction_supports_claim,
143	            "powered": powered,  # trusted caller flag, recorded for audit (#H-002)
144	        },
145	    )
146	    if not powered:
147	        return Verdict.indeterminate(hypothesis_id, rule, notes="underpowered", **common)
148	    if p_value <= alpha:
149	        if direction_supports_claim:
150	            return Verdict.supported(hypothesis_id, rule, **common)
151	        return Verdict.contradicted(hypothesis_id, rule, **common)
152	    return Verdict.indeterminate(hypothesis_id, rule, notes="not significant at alpha", **common)

2	
3	A clone registers its :class:`~assay_engine.contracts.study.StudyDefinition` factory under a
4	name; the engine looks it up to run. This is the only place adapters and the engine meet by
5	name, and even here the engine holds only a factory callable — it imports no adapter module
6	itself (ARCHITECTURE.md §3). The instance repository imports the engine, builds its study,
7	and registers it.

62	def _point_id(ref: str) -> str:
63	    """Deterministic Qdrant point UUID for an engine string id (Qdrant ids must be int/UUID)."""
64	    return str(uuid.uuid5(_POINT_NS, ref))

67	class QdrantVectorStore:
68	    """Reference :class:`VectorStore` over a loopback Qdrant collection (cosine).
69	
70	    The engine's ids are arbitrary strings; Qdrant point ids must be int/UUID, so each id is
71	    mapped to a deterministic UUID and the original id is stored in the point payload (and
72	    returned by ``query``). This is a goal-agnostic reference implementation; a study may
73	    supply its own ``VectorStore`` (collection naming, distance, sharding are study choices).
74	    """
75	
76	    def __init__(self, collection: str, dim: int, *, client: Any | None = None) -> None:
77	        self._collection = collection
78	        self._dim = dim
79	        # Track ownership: only close a client we created ourselves; an injected client is the
80	        # caller's to manage (#117).
81	        self._owns_client = client is None
82	        self._client = client if client is not None else get_qdrant_client()
83	
84	    def close(self) -> None:
85	        """Close the underlying client if this store created it (no-op for an injected one)."""
86	        if self._owns_client:
87	            close = getattr(self._client, "close", None)
88	            if callable(close):
89	                close()
90	
91	    def __enter__(self) -> "QdrantVectorStore":
92	        return self
93	
94	    def __exit__(self, *_exc: object) -> None:
95	        self.close()
96	
97	    def ensure_collection(self) -> None:
98	        from qdrant_client.models import Distance, VectorParams
99	
100	        if not self._client.collection_exists(self._collection):
101	            self._client.create_collection(
102	                self._collection,
103	                vectors_config=VectorParams(size=self._dim, distance=Distance.COSINE),
104	            )
105	
106	    def upsert(
107	        self, ids: Sequence[str], vectors: Sequence[Sequence[float]], *, batch_size: int = 256
108	    ) -> None:
109	        from qdrant_client.models import PointStruct
110	
111	        if len(ids) != len(vectors):
112	            raise ValueError("ids and vectors must have equal length")
113	        if batch_size < 1:
114	            raise ValueError("batch_size must be >= 1")
115	        # Build each batch of PointStructs lazily, so only one batch is materialized in memory at
116	        # a time (bounding BOTH payload AND memory) rather than boxing the whole corpus up front
117	        # (#118). Qdrant guidance: don't upload point-by-point, but bound the batch.
118	        for start in range(0, len(ids), batch_size):
119	            batch = [
120	                PointStruct(id=_point_id(ids[j]), vector=list(vectors[j]), payload={"ref": ids[j]})
121	                for j in range(start, min(start + batch_size, len(ids)))
122	            ]
123	            self._client.upsert(self._collection, points=batch)
124	
125	    def query(self, vector: Sequence[float], k: int) -> list[tuple[str, float]]:
126	        if k <= 0:
127	            raise ValueError(f"k must be a positive integer; got {k}")  # #H-021
128	        res = self._client.query_points(
129	            self._collection, query=list(vector), limit=k, with_payload=True
130	        )
131	        # A returned point without our 'ref' payload (e.g. inserted by another writer) would raise
132	        # an opaque KeyError/TypeError; skip such points defensively rather than crash the query
133	        # (#H-021). The store only ever upserts points carrying 'ref', so this is belt-and-braces.
134	        out: list[tuple[str, float]] = []
135	        for p in res.points:
136	            ref = (p.payload or {}).get("ref")
137	            if ref is not None:
138	                out.append((ref, float(p.score)))
139	        return out
